title: Add '--group-add keep-groups': supplementary groups into container
body:
Currently we have rootless users who want to leak their groups access
into containers, but this group access is only able to be pushed in by
a hard to find OCI Runtime annotation.  This PR makes this option a lot
more visable and hides the complexity within the podman client.

This option is only really needed for local rootless users. It makes
no sense for remote clients, and probably makes little sense for
rootfull containers.

Signed-off-by: Daniel J Walsh <dwalsh@redhat.com>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]".  That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.
-->

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

@mheon @vrothberg @baude @giuseppe WDYT?  This same issue has come up in multiple conversations, just today.  People are looking for this option and it is hidden in the crun man page noise.  I believe this is a very handy feature for rootless users and something we should make much more of a first class citizen.

@edsantiago I think we need to get clever about how to test this.  Basically need to setup a users group and a directory where the user launching podman only has access via group access.  Run one container without --groups have it fail, run same container with --groups option should succeed.
https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1784889 Is an example of this.

I just published a whole article on this.
https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/supplemental-groups-podman-containers

>I just wonder if bool is the right type

That's my initial reaction too: I can envision someone wanting to pass in `--groups=plugdev,cron` but not `wheel` (not that any files are ever owned by wheel, but, shrug).

Testing this is going to need some cleverness. It may require setup help from the framework, which means we'll have to coordinate between Cirrus, Fedora gating, and RHEL gating :(

@edsantiago, that's a nice example.  Maybe we can have something ~symmetrical to `--user` where `--user=keep-id` has a special meaning and maps the current user into the container?  `--groups=keep-all`?

We have to wait for @giuseppe to chime in, but I am not sure we can be that precise on the groups we want to leak.
I think crun is just not calling setgroups.

Looking at the man page this might be possible.
```
man setgroups
...
       int setgroups(size_t size, const gid_t *list);
```

> We have to wait for @giuseppe to chime in, but I am not sure we can be that precise on the groups we want to leak.
> I think crun is just not calling setgroups.
> 
> Looking at the man page this might be possible.

yes, crun just skips calling `setgroups` so the original groups are maintained.

Should it perhaps be something under `--security-opt`? Like `--security-opt skip-setgroups=1`?
@giuseppe Could we call the setgroups with specific groups to keep?

@giuseppe I am not crazy about  --security-opt? Like --security-opt skip-setgroups=1? since this is similar to --user and burying it under --security-opt just hides it.

I think we are seeing this is quite commonly needed.

I've probably spent too much time on this already... but I can't get this to work at all, not with the new `--group=1` flag, nor even with manually specifying `--annotation`:
```console
$ ls -ld gt;ls -ldn gt
drwxrwx---. 1 root esm 30 Feb 23 14:12 gt/
drwxrwx---. 1 0 14904 30 Feb 23 14:12 gt/
$ ./bin/podman run --annotation run.oci.keep_original_groups=1 --rm --mount type=bind,source=$(pwd)/gt,destination=/gt registry.access.redhat.com/ubi8/ubi ls -l /gt
ls: cannot open directory '/gt': Permission denied
```
I've tried with `--userns=keep-id`, with `-v` instead of `--mount`, tried different images. Tried `:Z` which of course fails instantly. 

crun-0.17-1.fc33 which, as best I can tell, includes 6df930821d (the change to run.oci.keep_original_groups) (0.11)

I'm probably missing something very obvious. Any help appreciated; otherwise I'll take a fresh look in the morning.
SELinux?

Is ESM your primary group?  Try adding a sumplimental group "test", then add esm to 'test' and then execute
groups to make sure you have access to the directory outside of container.

Finally make sure you are running with crun.

It's not SELinux. Or at least, not once I `chcon -t container_file_t /path/to/gt`. After that, if the `gt` directory is my primary group (`esm`), podman succeeds with or without the annotation. If I `chgrp` the directory to one of my secondary groups, I get EPERM but with no AVC (and it also fails with `setenforce 0`).

> @giuseppe Could we call the setgroups with specific groups to keep?

no we can't, we can either keep them all or just change to groups mapped in the user namespace.


> @giuseppe I am not crazy about --security-opt? Like --security-opt skip-setgroups=1? since this is similar to --user and burying it under --security-opt just hides it.

Personally I don't think it looks bad, we are already using security-opt for major features like seccomp and SELinux
I don't mine `--security-opt` for this. It reduces potential confusion with the existing group flags.
Ok I got out voted, switched man pages to reference `--security-opt keep-groups` Please review man pages to make sure everyone agrees.  Will work on changing the code, when I get some more time.

To continue bike shedding: I would prefer `--keep-groups` over `--security-opt` as I find the lines of security a bit blurry in this context; SELinux or seccomp are more unambiguous to me.  There is also ` --group-add` which we may take into account.  Having two, three, four different flags to alter group settings may be confusing to users _and_ smells risky to regress on.

Looking at `--user=keep-id` we could add a similar special value to `--group-add`?
Lets discuss at standup.

@rhatdan: PR needs rebase.

<details>

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes/test-infra](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>
Ugh. The problem is that `run_podman` is a bash function, and `sg` will have no idea how to run that. Let me cogitate.

Well, you want a group that exists in both host and container, with the same GID. I'd say `sys` is a good candidate.

You could `sg sys "$PODMAN ..."` but please include a big "DO NOT DO THIS" comment.

A hidden chroot(1) feature is the possibility to tweak UID/GID and additional groups before running a program (https://git.savannah.gnu.org/gitweb/?p=coreutils.git;a=commit;h=c45c51fe97193898f3909dcf5e4c0e117ab239a2):

```
# chroot --userspec=1000:1000 --groups 1,2,3 / id -G
1000 1 2 3
```
I think it makes easier to specify the numerical value so you don't have to worry if the group is defined on the host and in the container.
I switched to using chroot. Thanks @giuseppe

@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL
@giuseppe @vrothberg PTAL
LGTM
/lgtm

This is all the kernel allows,  We have an open request with the kernel to allow us to specify which Groups to leak, but it has not been accepted up stream.

@giuseppe Correct me if I am wrong.

when running in a user namespace, `setgroups` is possible only with the groups that are part of the user namespace and the user namespace was created with a setuid helper (newuidmap/newgidmap).  It is not possible to reference groups that are not mapped into the user namespace.

If the call to `setgroups` is skipped, then the original groups are maintained.  So it is possible to either maintain the existing groups or use groups that are part of the user namespace (if it is created with newuidmap/newgidmap).
